bio.fun_fact: She began skiing at age 2 and began racing at age 5
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Specialty: Women's Downhill
  • Daughter of David Wiles and Jennifer James
  • Has one brother, Steele
  • Began skiing at age 2 and began racing at age 5
  • Hobbies include riding bikes and motorcycles, cooking, and spending time outdoors
Biographical Information

Jacqueline Wiles learned to ski at roughly the same time she learned to walk. She grew up making weekend trips to White Pass Ski Club in Washington with her family, where she discovered what made skiing fun. A product of the Pacific Northwest Ski Association, the Aurora resident developed a natural talent and fearless ability to ski fast. After graduating from Canby High School, she faced a pivotal decision — attend college or pursue her Olympic dream. The choice was simple. She knew college would always be there, but she had only one moment to chase her goals.

Wiles burst onto the national scene in 2013 when she won the U.S. Alpine Championships downhill title at Copper Mountain. She defended that title in 2014, earning her spot on the roster for the Olympic Winter Games Sochi 2014 at age 21. She made her world cup debut in November 2013 and scored her first world cup points weeks before the Olympic Games with a 15th-place finish in Cortina d'Ampezzo, Italy — a venue that would become special throughout her career. The next few seasons proved challenging as she worked to establish herself on the circuit, but 2017 brought vindication when she earned her first world cup podium with a third-place finish in downhill at Altenmarkt-Zauchensee, Austria.

Her career took a devastating turn on Feb. 3, 2018, when she crashed during a world cup downhill in Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany, just before the Olympic Winter Games PyeongChang 2018. The crash was catastrophic — she tore her ACL, MCL, LCL and POL, injured her lateral meniscus and tib/fib joint, broke her fibula and suffered perennial nerve damage. Her father David, watching at 2 a.m. from home, received a phone call from a doctor on the course assuring him his daughter was okay. The injury required three surgeries over five months and sidelined her for nearly two years. She returned to downhill skis 17 months later, writing on Instagram about finally feeling like a racer again.

The comeback proved arduous. After missing PyeongChang 2018, Wiles fought through additional setbacks including a broken collarbone in 2020 and patella tendon tears in both knees that required more surgeries. She battled PTSD from her crashes, which manifested as a defensive skiing style and overwhelming fear. Working with a psychologist, she learned to acknowledge the fear without letting it control her. In 2016, teammate Lindsey Vonn named Wiles the first-ever Lindsey Vonn Foundation athlete ambassador, providing the crucial financial support necessary for adequate and holistic recovery. The two developed a deep friendship built on mutual respect and understanding of the sport's demands.

The 2023-24 season marked Wiles' triumphant return to elite form. At age 31, she achieved a career-best second-place finish in downhill at Cortina d'Ampezzo — her third podium at the Italian resort and her first since 2018. 

During her recovery years, she discovered a passion for flying and earned her private pilot's license with support from Textron Aviation, finding parallels between aviation and ski racing in the calculation, risk and need to plan ahead. When not training, she rides motorcycles and plays trumpet with her father every Fourth of July during their local flag-raising ceremony on the Oregon coast.

Olympic Experience
  • 3-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 bronze)
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, bronze (Team Alpine Combined - Women), 4th (Downhill - Women), 13th (Super G - Women)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, 21th (Downhill - Women)
    • Olympic Games Sochi 2014, 26th (Downhill - Women)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2025 – (Downhill - Women), 10th (Team Alpine Combined - Women)
  • Years of participation: Downhill - Women 2017, 2021, 2025; Team Alpine Combined - Women 2025; Super G - Women 2017, 2021; Super Combined - Women 2015
  • Top finish: – 2017 (Super G - Women), 2025 (Downhill - Women)

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Son of Rolf and Wendy Wilimovsky
  • Has one younger brother, Alec
  • Started swimming at age 9 because he was declined admission to a summer camp due to his inability to swim a 1:50 in the 100-yard freestyle
  • Played water polo and surfed before committing to swimming
  • Hobbies include reading, going to the beach, surfing and spending time with his dog, Hurley
  • Aspires to go to business school.

    • Olympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 4th (1500m Freestyle - Men), 5th (10km Freestyle - Men)

  • Years of participation: 10km Freestyle - Men 2015, 2017, 2019; 5km - Team Relay - Mixed 2017, 2019; 5km - Team Time Trial - Mixed 2015; 25km Freestyle - Men 2013

    • Silver – 2017 (10km Freestyle - Men, 5km - Team Relay - Mixed)

Quick Facts
  • Personal: Daughter of Edward and Linda Wilkinson
  • Personal: has one brother, Robert (born 6/4/73)
  • Personal: married Eriek Hulseman on Sept. 7, 2002
  • Personal: nicknames are La-La and Wilkie
  • Personal: Laura greatly appreciates the support she receives from her family
  • Personal: has two dogs and two cats
  • Personal: enjoys  drawing, writing, reading and making collages
  • Personal: Prior to the Sydney Olympics, she made a collage with pictures and cutouts from magazines that inspired her or made her laugh and she's started cutting up magazines for a collage to inspire her going into the Beijing Games
  • Personal: volunteers with children's organizations and does speaking engagements
  • Personal: a gymnast for seven years
  • Personal: has been diving since age 15
  • Personal: enjoys all of her dives
  • Personal: trains six or more hours a day, six days a week
  • Personal: listens to music or plays cards, talks and laughs between dives during a contest
  • Personal: before every dive recites Philippians 4:13, "I can do all things through Christ, who strengthens me."
  • Personal: has her own web site, www.laurawilkinson.com
  • Personal: Likes to watch the movie "Miracle" for inspiration before big meets.

bio.education: Driftwood Academy (Lake Park, Fla.) ‘98 Indiana University East ‘15, Business Administration
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Daughter of Richard Williams and Oracene Price
  • Has four sisters, Serena, Lyndrea, Isha and Yetunde (deceased in 2003)
  • Opened her own clothing line, EleVen, and wears the tennis apparel during matches
  • Founded V Starr Interiors, an interior design company in Palm Beach Gardens and Jupiter, Florida, which specializes in residential design
  • Designer of the Venus Williams Collection, a line of women's leather apparel for Wilson's Leather
  • Loves horror movies even though they frighten her
  • Minority owner of the Miami Dolphins
  • Withdrew from 2011 US Open after being diagnosed with Sjogren’s Syndrome
  • Attended the Art Institute of Fort Lauderdale and earned a degree in fashion design in 2007
  • Interests include fashion and interior design.
Olympic Experience
  • 5-time Olympian; 5-time Olympic medalist (4 gold, 1 silver)
    • Olympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, silver (Doubles - Mixed), 17th (Doubles - Women), 33th (Singles - Women)
    • Olympic Games London 2012, gold (Doubles - Women), 9th (Singles - Women)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2008, gold (Doubles - Women), 5th (Singles - Women)
    • Olympic Games Athens 2004, 9th (Singles - Women), 17th (Doubles - Women)
    • Olympic Games Sydney 2000, gold (Doubles - Women, Singles - Women)

Quick Facts
  • Son of Patricia Edwards and George Williams
  • Has two sisters, Janay and Jianna and three brothers, Gerome, Josiah, and Jacob
  • Hobbies include family time, writing, shoe and cologne shopping and live music.
Paralympic Experience
  • Three-time Paralympian; Three-time Paralympic medalist guide runner (3 silver)
  • Paralympic Games 2020
  • Paralympic Games Rio 2016, silver (long jump)
  • Paralympic Games London 2012, silver (long jump), 4th (triple jump)
  • Paralympic Games Beijing 2008, silver (long jump), 5th (triple jump)
World Championship Experience

  • Years of Participation: 2013, 2015, 2017, 2019
  • Medals: 6 (4 gold, 2 silver)
  • Gold – 2019 (long jump), 2017 (long jump), 2015 (long jump), 2013 (long jump)
  • Silver – 2013 (triple jump), 2006 (long jump)

Career Highlights

USA Basketball

Quick Facts
  • Parents are Trey and Janna Williams
  • Has an older brother named Trent
  • Parents are her most admired because, "They are a true example of a strong, God-fearing couple. My dad is also a cancer survivor and my mom bravely stood by both our sides"
  • Was diagnosed with Ewings Sarcoma in Feb. 29, 2016 (the week of her 19th birthday). Was given three surgery options and rotationplasty was the best option for her to have an active, healthy life
  • Happiest moment in life was July 7, 2016 when she had her amputation because "it opened up so many amazing doors." Also favors Jan. 10, 2017, the day of her last chemotherapy treatment
  • Has started a foundation called LIve n Leap that grants needs and dreams to adolescent and young-adult patients (ages 18-29) with life-threatening illnesses. "I made it a goal to make sure no other patient felt the way I did when I was told there are not any nationwide philanthropic foundations that benefited AYA patients in this way"
  • Played volleyball for one semester at Texas Lutheran University before being diagnosed with cancer
  • Married Kyle Coffee in 2020
  • Works as a MAKO product specialist at Stryker Corporation
  • Hobbies include hunting, fishing, camping with her family, playing with her goldendoodle Olive, and spending time with family and friends.
Paralympic Experience

    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, gold (Team - Women)
World Championship Experience

  • Years of participation: Team - Women 2018
  • Medals: 1 (1 silver)
    • Silver – 2018 (Team - Women)

USA Track and Field

Quick Facts

  • Son of Carson and Lisa. Has five siblings – Heather, Elizabeth, David, Lorna and Alex.

  • Was hit by a car while riding his bike, causing him to be paralyzed at the age of 16
  • While in the hospital, Chris Okon, a player from the University of Wisconsin-Whitewater, came to visit him and gave him the contact information to a local coach.
  • Started playing wheelchair basketball just four months after his accident
  • Played collegiate basketball at the University of Wisconsin-Whitewater
  • One of his favorite quotes is, “People say not to bite off more than you can chew. I would rather choke on greatness than nibble on mediocrity.”
